data.title: Stock Market News Today, 2/18/26 – U.S. Stock Futures Climb Ahead of FOMC Minutes
data.description:
U.S. stock futures climbed early Wednesday as traders anticipated the minutes from the Federal Reserve's January meeting. Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the S&P 500 (SPX), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) were up 0.69%, 0.61%, and 0.51%, respectively, at 5:46 a.m. EST on February 18.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

During Tuesday's regular trading, the three major indexes closed higher.  The S&P 500, Dow, and Nasdaq Composite all edged up roughly 0.1% on the day. The software sector, battered by AI disruption fears, dropped further yesterday. CrowdStrike (CRWD) tumbled 3.6%, while ServiceNow (NOW) shed 1.1%.

Turning to economic data, traders are focused on the FOMC minutes due later today. Also, data on housing starts, building permits, and durable goods orders will be published today. Of more importance is Friday's release of the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) index for December, the Fed's favorite gauge, which will reveal more about the economy's health.

On the earnings front, Booking Holdings (BKNG), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), Analog Devices (ADI), and Carvana (CVNA) will release their results today.

Notably, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up, floating near 4.07%. WTI crude oil futures were trending higher, hovering near $62.61 per barrel as of the last check. Additionally, the Gold Spot U.S. dollar price decreased to nearly $4,913 per ounce on Wednesday.

Elsewhere, European indexes opened higher today amid positive global market developments. Moreover, the UK's inflation rate fell to 3% in January as per official stats.

Asia-Pacific Markets Traded Higher

Asia-Pacific markets traded higher on February 18, with Mainland Chinese and Hong Kong markets remaining closed for the Lunar New Year.

Meanwhile, Japan's Nikkei gained 1.02%, and the Topix increased 1.21%.

data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: DJI's Turbulent Chapter: Bans, Breakthroughs, and a Breach
data.description:
DJI is a Chinese company founded in 2006 in Shenzhen, China. It began as a small startup producing flight controllers for model helicopters. Today, it produces cameras and is the undisputed global leader in civilian drone manufacturing, controlling roughly 70% of the consumer drone market and supplying critical technology to emergency responders, infrastructure inspectors, agricultural operators, and content creators across more than 190 countries.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The opening months of 2026, however, captured the full complexity of DJI's position: a landmark regulatory blow in the United States, a milestone certification in Brazil, a meaningful product refresh, and a security vulnerability that exposed thousands of homes to unauthorized surveillance. Each development tells a different story about where DJI stands. Together, they reveal a company navigating extraordinary pressure with mixed results.

The Day America Decided DJI Was Too Chinese to Fly

First, the decision did not arrive without warning. In December 2025, the Federal Communications Commission formally added DJI and other foreign drone manufacturers to its Covered List, a designation that makes it impossible for new models to receive FCC authorization, effectively barring their import and sale in the United States.

The decision, framed by FCC Chairman Brendan Carr, was followed by an executive order signed by President Trump in June 2025. This document aims to accelerate domestic drone production and shield the industry from foreign control or exploitation. According to the White House, the decision was based on the fact that foreign-manufactured drones and components could enable persistent surveillance, data exfiltration, and destructive operations on American soil. To put it differently, Washington was not merely acting on the grounds of trade competition. It was, at least in its own framing, acting on national security concerns.

The move was years in the making. The U.S. Army banned DJI drones in 2017. The Commerce Department placed the private company on its economic blacklist in 2020. And in 2022, the Department of Defense linked DJI to the People's Liberation Army of China. December 2025 was, consequently, the culmination of a sustained, bipartisan effort to push the company out of the American market, not a sudden shift, but the final step in a long march toward exclusion.

For DJI, the implications are significant. The company's drones are used by civilian hobbyists, public safety agencies, agricultural operators, and film productions across the country. Its global head of policy, Adam Welsh, signaled willingness to cooperate with U.S. authorities, and yet the FCC's decision came without disclosing what specific intelligence had informed it, a lack of transparency that DJI publicly criticized. As a matter of fact, DJI's spokesperson noted that no information was shared about which data or assessments the Executive had relied upon to reach its conclusion.

Now, it is important to realize that the ban is not a kill switch. Existing drones with prior FCC authorization remain legal to own, fly, and sell secondhand. The FAA has issued no flight restrictions, and operators are not being asked to ground their fleets. What changes, however, is the future. Commercial operators, public safety departments, and inspection firms should not assume they will be able to purchase additional DJI units, since retail availability will shrink as inventory depletes without replenishment. Not only will repair services and spare parts grow scarce, but also warranty support may become increasingly difficult to access over time. Firmware updates received a temporary lifeline when, in January 2026, the FCC issued an exemption allowing existing devices to receive security patches at least through January 1, 2027, though the long-term picture nonetheless remains deeply uncertain.

The market gap DJI leaves behind is real. American drone manufacturers will compete to fill it, and sooner or later, one or more will emerge to capture a meaningful share of what DJI once owned. Whether any can match its price-to-performance ratio in the near term, however, remains an open question.

Brazil Gives DJI the Green Light Washington Refused to Shine

While Washington moved to restrict DJI, Brazil, conversely, took the opposite direction. On January 21, 2026, Brazil's civil aviation authority, ANAC, granted an Airworthiness Project Authorization for BVLOS operations, that is to say, flights conducted Beyond Visual Line of Sight without a human operator maintaining direct visual contact with the drone.

The significance of this certification extends far beyond Brazilian airspace. In fact, ANAC is one of the world's most technically rigorous aviation regulators, and its approval carries weight precisely because it is not easily obtained. By this decision, ANAC is effectively signaling that the systems meet a high, independently verified safety standard. With this in mind, other regulatory bodies around the world might approve the same tools of the big company. 

Before this authorization, companies seeking to conduct BVLOS operations in Brazil faced a cumbersome process. The approval period was just to check some particular topics, self-certifying each dock system, and demonstrating airworthiness criteria, for example. However, the new framework dramatically compresses that timeline. In other words, what once took months of regulatory friction can now, in principle, be resolved in a fraction of the time, provided that operators meet the remaining conditions.

And those conditions, it must be said, remain meaningful. Commercial registrations must be in order under Brazilian law, remote pilots must hold appropriate qualifications, and each mission requires a site-specific risk assessment, and flight plans must be submitted through Brazil's SARPAS system with an active human decision-maker monitoring operations. Additionally, individual aircraft require their own CAER certification, though this certificate can be transferred, meaning a pre-approved system can be passed to a service provider or enterprise team already set up for deployment.

Given these points, the broader implication is clear. Brazil is not the last country that will grant this level of certification to DJI's enterprise drone line. It may well be the first of many, and regulators around the world will be watching closely. In the long run, the Brazil decision may prove more consequential for DJI's commercial future than the American ban, particularly in markets where the geopolitical pressure that shaped Washington's decision simply does not apply.

The Firmware Update That Turned a Camera Into a Creator's Best Friend

Away from the regulatory battlefield, and in sharp contrast to the legal complexity unfolding elsewhere, DJI delivered a piece of news that follows a market trend.

The Chinese company made an update to its camera, Osmo 360. The difference is based on adding native vertical video and high video quality. Moreover, Single Lens mode now supports live streaming, and a new Film Tone feature introduces six cinematic color filters designed for direct-to-platform publication. For content creators who shoot for TikTok, Instagram Reels, or YouTube Shorts, the update removes a longstanding friction point: the need to crop, reformat, or re-encode footage before posting. In essence, the Osmo 360 now shoots, processes, and publishes in a single uninterrupted workflow, which is, specifically, what the modern creator economy demands.

In the meantime, amid a period dominated by legal and security headlines, the Osmo update serves as a reminder that DJI's consumer product engine continues to operate at a high cadence, regardless of the turbulence surrounding it. Generally speaking, this is a company that rarely slows its product development pace, even when its legal and regulatory environment is under considerable strain.

A Hobbyist With a PS5 Controller Who Ended Up Inside 7,000 Strangers' Homes

Nonetheless, the most alarming development of this period did not come from Washington or Brasília. It came from a hobbyist trying to connect his DJI Romo robot vacuum to a Sony's (SONY) device, a PlayStation 5 controller.

Sammy Azdoufal, while reverse-engineering Romo's protocols with the assistance of AI tools, discovered that his custom application, once connected to DJI's servers, was recognized as the authorized controller for approximately 7,000 Romo units around the world. He could, accordingly, activate them remotely, view live camera feeds, listen through their microphones, access detailed 2D floor maps of their owners' homes, monitor battery status, and determine each device's approximate physical location through its IP address. Every three seconds, device data is streamed to DJI's servers, and any authenticated client, without proper access controls, could subscribe to those global data channels and read it all. To clearly outline the exposure: visual access, audio access, spatial mapping, location tracking, and real-time device status, all accessible to an unauthorized party, all simultaneously.

The underlying flaw was in the backend permission validation layer governing the MQTT messaging protocol used to relay data between devices and servers. There was no meaningful access segmentation: a single authenticated session could access data from all connected devices. Even more critically, the vulnerability bypassed user-set PIN codes designed to restrict camera access, rendering a core privacy control entirely ineffective. In other words, the lock was on the door, but the door had no walls beside it.

DJI's initial public response was that the problem had already been resolved. That statement, as it turned out, was premature. The patch was deployed in two phases, on February 8 and February 10, 2026, and reporting confirmed that the vulnerability remained exploitable between those dates, leaving devices exposed for days after the company's first assurance. DJI subsequently acknowledged the error as a permission validation failure in the backend, confirmed the fix was fully applied, and stated that no evidence of malicious exploitation had been identified. Even so, the damage to confidence was already done.

Above all, the episode carries weight that goes beyond the technical details. DJI's Romo is the company's first consumer home robot, a product category defined by its intimate presence inside people's most private spaces. A security failure of this nature, affecting thousands of devices simultaneously, and disclosed only after an independent researcher went public, is precisely the kind of incident that regulators in Washington point to when questioning whether Chinese-manufactured technology can be trusted in American homes and critical environments. The timing, coinciding with the broader climate of regulatory scrutiny, could not, in the final analysis, have been worse.

Seventy Percent of the Sky, and Still Nowhere to Land

All things considered, what emerges from this period is not a portrait of a company in crisis, but of a company in tension. DJI remains the undisputed technology leader in commercial and consumer drones, trusted by professionals across dozens of industries, with an engineering output that few rivals can match. Its regulatory milestone in Brazil demonstrates that institutional credibility, even under political pressure, remains within reach. On the other hand, the security failure with the Romo, the regulatory exile from the world's largest economy, and the unresolved question of data sovereignty all point to the same underlying challenge: trust, once eroded, is difficult to rebuild, particularly when the political environment is actively working against it.

The ban will reshape American drone markets. The Brazil certification will open new commercial corridors. The Osmo update will sell cameras. The Romo vulnerability, however, will be remembered as the moment DJI's ambitions in home robotics collided, painfully, with the scrutiny that now follows every product the company releases. On the whole, DJI enters this new chapter with more momentum than its critics would admit, and more vulnerability than its supporters would prefer to acknowledge.

Whether DJI can navigate that scrutiny, technically, legally, and reputationally, will ultimately determine whether the story of this period reads, in retrospect, as a turning point or merely as turbulence on a longer journey that is still very much underway.
data.author: Daniel Nigri Miranda

data.title: CoreWeave Stock Forecast Ahead of Earnings – Here's What to Expect
data.description:
CoreWeave (CRWV) will release its fourth-quarter fiscal 2025 results after markets close on February 26. The cloud infrastructure provider has seen sharp volatility amid recent AI fears, with shares down 4.4% over the past month. CoreWeave specializes in high-performance Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs (graphics processing units) for training large AI models and serves large clients like OpenAI and Meta (META) across 33+ data centers.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Execution risk remains the prime concern for the neo-cloud provider. Investors will scrutinize management comments for signs of further delays in AI infrastructure buildouts. In Q3, summer weather halted progress at its Denton, Texas data center, forcing cuts to 2025 capex and revenue forecasts.

Expectations from CoreWeave

The Street expects CoreWeave to post an adjusted loss of $0.50 per share, significantly wider than $0.08 per share reported last year. Sales are forecast to surge about 105% year-over-year to $1.53 billion. However, compared to Q3FY25, the figure shows a modest rise of 12.5%.

Meanwhile, the company has guided for full-year 2025 sales of $5.05-5.15 billion and adjusted EBIT (earnings before interest and taxes) between $690 and $720 million.

Analysts' Views Ahead of Results

Citizens JMP analyst Gregory Miller reiterated his Buy rating and $180 price target, implying 97.8% upside potential. He stated that CoreWeave leads in renting out powerful GPUs as a service (GPUaaS) and is well-placed to meet booming AI infrastructure needs, with multi-year deals and over $55.6 billion in revenue backlog. The market for GPU rentals is exploding due to faster Gen AI adoption and big cloud companies outsourcing more.

Risks like falling prices, customer concentration, and high debt persist. Still, CoreWeave's top market position, clear future contracts, and growth potential make CRWV a strong short- to medium-term AI infrastructure play.

Similarly, D.A. Davidson analyst Alex Platt kept his Buy rating and $110 price target, suggesting 20.9% upside, after Nvidia infused another $2 billion in CoreWeave. Nvidia is helping CoreWeave reduce risks in building 5 GW of data centers by 2030, supporting massive compute demand and sustainable growth.

Is CRWV Stock a Good Buy?

On TipRanks, CoreWeave has a Moderate Buy consensus rating based on nine Buys and eight Hold ratings. The average CoreWeave price target of $118.57 implies 30.3% upside potential from current levels. Over the past year, CRWV shares have skyrocketed over 145%.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: Microsoft (MSFT) Bets Big on the Global South With $50 Billion AI Buildout Through 2030
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) is making a major push to bring AI tools and data centers into developing markets over the rest of this decade. The company said it is on track to invest about $50 billion across the so-called Global South by 2030. The announcement came this week at an AI summit in New Delhi, where global tech leaders met with government officials.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Microsoft described the effort as a way to narrow what it sees as a growing gap in AI use between richer nations and lower-income countries. The company said it is investing to help more regions build the basic systems needed to adopt AI at scale.

Meanwhile, MSFT shares dropped 1.11% on Tuesday, closing at $396.86.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: 'Branches Still Drive Deposits': JPMorgan (JPM) Doubles Down on Physical Banking
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM), the largest U.S. bank by assets, is preparing a major expansion of its physical banking footprint, signaling that traditional branches still matter in the digital era.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The bank plans to open more than 160 new branches across over 30 states in 2026, part of a broader multiyear investment program. The effort follows a 2024 commitment to open more than 500 locations within three years under its Chase consumer banking brand.

The move stands out at a time when many investors assumed mobile apps would eventually replace physical branches.

JPMorgan Aims to Win Deposits and Customers

JPMorgan is trying to grow its share of U.S. retail deposits, and local branches remain one of the most reliable ways to attract new customers. The bank expects new locations to become profitable within about four years. Management said some branches are reaching that goal even sooner as they add credit-card users and wealth clients.

More broadly, banks have found that people are more likely to open an account when they live near a branch, even if they later manage most of their banking online. The branch builds trust, while mobile and digital tools handle everyday transactions.

Other Banks Also Expand Branch Networks

JPMorgan is not alone. Other lenders are also expanding physical networks instead of closing them.

For instance, Bank of America (BAC) has been opening new financial centers in cities such as Denver and Philadelphia and plans dozens more locations over the next few years. Regional banks are taking similar steps as they try to attract steady, low-cost deposits.

Even as digital banking grows, physical locations still matter for customer relationships, lending, and wealth services. Many customers, including younger ones, still prefer having a branch nearby for security and confidence.

Is JPM a Buy, Hold, or Sell? 

On Wall Street, JPMorgan Chase's shares remain a Moderate Buy based on analysts' consensus rating. This breaks down to 12 Buys and nine Holds assigned by 21 analysts over the past three months. Moreover, the average JPM price target of $346.53 implies more than 12% upside from current trading levels.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Anthropic's $80 Billion Cloud Bet Collides With Pentagon Demands
data.description:
Anthropic just closed a $30 billion funding round with top-tier backers. However, behind the fresh capital sits a new tension that could shape its future in both Washington and Silicon Valley.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The Wall Street Journal reports that the Pentagon is reviewing its ties with Anthropic over the limits the company places on how its Claude AI model can be used. At the same time, The Information reveals that Anthropic expects to pay Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT) at least $80 billion through 2029 just to run Claude on their cloud systems.

Together, these two threads show a company that is both politically exposed and financially dependent on Big Tech.

Pentagon Pressure Meets AI Guardrails

Anthropic is the maker of Claude, which is cleared for use in classified settings. That status helped it secure a defense contract worth up to $200 million and form a partnership with Palantir Technologies (PLTR).

Yet friction has emerged over how freely the Defense Department can deploy the model. The Pentagon wants access to AI tools for all lawful purposes. Anthropic has pushed back on certain uses, including domestic surveillance and autonomous lethal activity.

Emil Michael, the undersecretary of war for research and engineering, put it plainly. "We have to be able to use any model for all lawful use cases. If any one company doesn't want to accommodate that, that's a problem for us," he said.

Moreover, a senior defense official said some inside the Pentagon now see Anthropic as a supply chain risk. That label is often applied to foreign threats, not to U.S. firms. The department may even ask vendors to certify that they do not use Claude.

Anthropic says it is "committed to using frontier AI in support of U.S. national security" and that it is holding "productive conversations" with the Pentagon.

Still, the dispute highlights a real split. Rivals such as OpenAI, Google, and xAI have agreed in principle to allow their models to be used in any lawful setting. Anthropic has not.

The $80 Billion Cloud Bill

In the meantime, Anthropic's business model ties it deeply to the very tech giants it competes alongside. According to The Information, Anthropic expects to pay Amazon, Google, and Microsoft at least $80 billion through 2029 to run Claude on their cloud servers. In addition, the company may spend as much as $100 billion more over that period to train its models.

That is only part of the story. When those cloud firms resell Claude to their own clients, they also take a cut of the revenue. Those resale payouts are rising fast. Anthropic paid about $1.3 million in 2024 to cloud providers as their share of AI sales. That figure was expected to jump to about $360 million last year, $1.9 billion this year, and $6.4 billion next year, based on its most optimistic forecasts.

The report estimates that those payouts could amount to roughly 10% of Anthropic's total revenue in those years. In one case, about 50% of Anthropic's gross profit from AI sales via Amazon has gone back to Amazon, according to people familiar with the arrangement. Gross profit here means revenue after paying for cloud infrastructure.

Amazon Web Services described the tie as a "differentiated partnership" and said it serves as Anthropic's primary cloud and training partner. Google typically takes between 20% and 30% of net revenue when reselling partner software, though the exact split for Claude is unclear. Microsoft, which committed to investing $5 billion in Anthropic, also receives a share of the revenue when it resells the model.

This structure gives cloud firms a strong incentive to promote Claude. For example, Microsoft has told Azure sales staff that selling Anthropic models counts toward their quotas just like Microsoft software and OpenAI models. However, it also means Anthropic's margins depend heavily on its partners. The more it scales, the more it pays.

For comparison, OpenAI pays 20% of its total revenue to Microsoft under its agreement. OpenAI has projected more than $13 billion in revenue share payments, mainly to Microsoft, this year and next.

What This Means for Investors

Anthropic is growing fast and projects revenue of up to $18 billion this year. Yet its path is shaped by two powerful forces. First, in Washington, access to defense work may hinge on how flexible it is with model use. Second, in Silicon Valley, its economics are intertwined with Amazon, Google, and Microsoft, which collect both infrastructure fees and revenue share.

We used TipRanks' Comparison Tool to compare notable publicly traded companies that employ chatbots similar to ChatGPT, Grok, and Claude. It's an excellent tool to gain a broader perspective on each stock and the AI industry as a whole.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: Bayer Stock Gains on Proposed $7.25B Settlement of Roundup Cancer Litigation
data.description:
Bayer's (BAYRY) U.S.-listed shares rose 6% on Tuesday after the German pharma and agriculture giant announced a proposed $7.25 billion settlement to resolve lawsuits alleging that the company's Roundup weedkiller caused cancer. The company's Frankfurt-listed shares (DE:BAYN) gained 7.4% yesterday.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Bayer has been caught up in the Roundup litigation since the completion of its $63 billion acquisition of Monsanto in 2018. The company has been facing allegations that glyphosate, the key ingredient in Roundup, causes cancer. However, Bayer has denied these accusations, saying that the Environmental Protection Agency and other regulators have determined that the product is safe to use.

More on Bayer's Proposed Roundup Settlement

Bayer disclosed that law firms representing tens of thousands of plaintiffs filed a motion on Tuesday seeking preliminary approval of the settlement in the Circuit Court of the City of St. Louis, Missouri. The settlement follows the Supreme Court's decision to hear Bayer's challenge to the Roundup litigation.

In January 2026, the Supreme Court agreed to hear an appeal from Bayer, which argued that consumers shouldn't be able to sue the company for failing to warn about cancer risks, because U.S. federal regulators had determined that glyphosate doesn't require a cancer warning. The court will hear arguments in the case in Spring 2026, with a ruling expected by early July. If Bayer wins, it could result in the dismissal of thousands of cases against the company. 

Bayer CEO Bill Anderson said that the proposed settlement, along with the Supreme Court case, "provides an essential path out of the litigation uncertainty." Given the proposed settlement, Bayer expects its provisions and litigation liabilities to increase from 7.8 billion euros to 11.8 billion euros. Also, the company expects around 5 billion euros in litigation‑related payments in 2026. Consequently, Bayer expects to report negative free cash flow for the year.

The company will now announce its 2025 results and 2026 guidance on March 4 ‌to reflect the settlement in its financial statements.

Is Bayer a Good Stock to Buy?

Wall Street has a Moderate Buy consensus rating on Bayer stock based on one Buy and one Hold rating. The average BAYRY stock price target of $12.35 indicates 14.2% downside risk. BAYRY stock has rallied 155% over the past year.
data.author: Sirisha Bhogaraju

data.title: Apple Stock Selloff 'Unwarranted' – Wedbush's Dan Ives Sticks to Street-High $350 PT
data.description:
Wedbush's top analyst Daniel Ives called Apple's (AAPL) recent stock selloff "unwarranted" and maintained his Street-high price target of $350, implying 32.6% upside potential. The longtime Apple bull dismissed concerns about the Siri AI delay, focusing on "Apple getting its AI strategy right." After an 8% five-day drop on delay news, AAPL rebounded 3.2% yesterday after Ives' upbeat note.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Ives warned that the market is "significantly underestimating" the transformative potential of Apple's upcoming Siri AI platform, slated for 2026. With a massive installed base of 2.5 billion iOS devices and 1.5 billion iPhones, Ives projects $75–$100 per-share value from AI monetization in coming years.

Ives ranks #636 out of the 12,108 analysts tracked on TipRanks. He has a 53% success rate and an average return per rating of 13.40%.

Ives Says 'This Time Is Different'

Ives acknowledged that investors "have endured this soap opera over the last year with Cupertino's lack of AI strategy," especially as rivals like Google (GOOGL) and Microsoft (MSFT) raced ahead with generative AI tools. Even so, he views the current panic overdone, emphasizing that Apple's advanced features are still on track for a summer 2026 rollout.

He stressed that this year's scenario is "way different" from last year's missteps. He highlighted Apple's partnership with Google Gemini, the addition of top external AI talent, and the internal recognition that its overhauled Siri architecture ranks among the company's most pivotal tech launches ever.

Ives noted that developers and consumers are "waiting patiently" for the enhanced Siri's phased rollouts, dubbing it "Apple's answer to ChatGPT and Perplexity." He also anticipates an AI-powered subscription service launching by autumn.

Is Apple a Good Stock to Buy Right Now?

On TipRanks, Apple stock has a Moderate Buy consensus rating based on 17 Buys, nine Holds, and one Sell rating. The average Apple price target of $307.55 implies 16.6% upside potential from current levels. Over the past year, AAPL shares have gained nearly 8%.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth
